title: The name that is not on the page
why.no-title: There is no name in the tab. Open any site and look at the tab at the top — something is written there. On yours, nothing is. The tag goes in the <head>.
why.title-not-closed: You opened <title> but never closed it. </title> is missing.
why.no-description: There is no explanation for search engines. Without it Google makes one up from the first words it finds on the page — sometimes that is the menu, sometimes "Accept cookies". This is the only thing persuading a person to click, and you left it to chance.
why.no-content: The description tag is there, but it is empty. content= with a real sentence inside is missing.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed, or closed in the wrong order.
blocks.0.text: Open any site and look at the very top — at the tab itself. Something is written there. It is not part of the page, yet there it is.
blocks.1.text: Your page has no such name yet. Its tab probably says something meaningless, or nothing at all.
blocks.4.text: Inside <head> — that is, in the invisible part. Logical: this is not the page's content, it is information ABOUT it.
blocks.5.text: Why it is not a trifle
blocks.6.text: This text goes to three places you do not think about: the tab label, bookmarks (when someone saves the page) and Google results. So it is the first thing a stranger sees about your page — before they even open it.
blocks.7.text: Put it on your page. Run. Nothing will change on screen — but look at the preview's tab.
blocks.8.text: The second thing in this course that is invisible yet matters. Get used to it — the web is full of them.
blocks.10.text: Go back to Google and look at one result more closely. It has TWO parts. The blue heading on top — that is the <title>, you know it now. And below it, in small grey letters, a sentence explaining what the page is about.
blocks.11.text: Where does that come from? You never wrote it.
blocks.12.text: If you did not write it — Google makes it up. It grabs the first words it finds on the page. Sometimes that is the menu. Sometimes it is "Accept cookies". Sometimes it is half a sentence, cut off mid-word.
blocks.13.text: That is the only thing persuading a person to click. And you left it to chance.
blocks.15.text: <meta> is another tag with no closing. It holds nothing — it states something. Here it states: this is the explanation of this page, written by me, not guessed.
blocks.17.text: Above are the two versions. The same site. One invites, the other repels.
blocks.18.text: Write it for a human, not a machine. One or two sentences, up to about a hundred and fifty characters — longer and Google cuts it off. Say what the person will find inside and why it is worth it.
blocks.19.text: Your turn. Give your page a name and an explanation.
